Stencil testing is used to discard fragments based on a configurable stencil mask.

To write maximum-performance systems software, you must treat RAM not as a flat, uniform array of bytes, but as a deeply pipelined, highly volatile hardware topology. Hardware optimizations (like CPU caches, prefetchers, and memory controllers) can accelerate your code by orders of magnitude—or cripple it through cache thrashing, alignment faults, and bus contention.
Secondary storage (or auxiliary memory) is non-volatile. Volatile means likely to change suddenly, unexpectedly, or uncontrollably.

A class is a definition of an object. It describes the algorithms, the data, it's format and relations to other classes. An object is a concrete instantiation of a class. A class is a user-defined type.

Operator Name Logic Resulting Bit is 1 if & AND a & b both bits are 1. | OR a | b at least one bit is 1. ^ XOR a ^ b the bits are different. ~ NOT ~a the input bit is 0 (it flips the bit). << Left Shift a << n Shifts bits to the left adding 0s to the right. >> Right Shift a >> n Shifts bits to the right discarding bits on the right.

If you're working in powers of two you can make use of bitwise operations to avoid expensive modulus and division operations This works only if n is a power of two #include <bit> #include <cstdint> // Suppose we want to multiply/divide/modulo by 16 constexpr uint64_t N = 16; // The power of two value static_assert(std::has_single_bit(N), "N must be a power of two"); template <uint64_t T> concept PowerOfTwo = std::has_single_bit(T); // divide: a / N uint64_t divide(uint64_t a) { return a >> std::countr_zero(N); } // multiply: a * N uint64_t multiply(uint64_t a) { return a << std::countr_zero(N); } // modulus: a % N uint64_t modulus(uint64_t a) { return a & (N - 1); } // Get the next multiple of n template <uint64_t N> requires PowerOfTwo<N> uint64_t nextMultipleOf(uint64_t value) { return (value + N - 1) & ~(N - 1); } Operation Standard Arithmetic Bitwise Equivalent Logic Divide a / 2^n a >> n Shifting right n times is mathematically identical to floor division by 2n. Multiply a * 2^n a << n Shifting left n times adds zeros Modulus a % 2^n a & (2^n - 1) Masking with 2n−1 keeps only the remainder bits.
void on(uint64_t &mask, uint32_t n) { mask |= 1ULL << n; }
void off(uint64_t &mask, uint32_t n) { mask &= ~(1ULL << n); }
void toggle(uint64_t &mask, uint32_t n) { mask ^= 1ULL << n; }
bool isOn(uint64_t mask, uint32_t n) { return (mask & (1ULL << n)) != 0; }

documents.t: If the LSB is on, the number is odd, otherwise it's even. bool isOdd(uint64_t mask, uint32_t n) { return (mask & 1ULL) == 1; } Notice how I used 1ULL, it's because I use a 64 bit unsigned integer as the mask.

documents.t: Static cast is used when you know the type of the object. It has no performance overhead. There are two type of casts: downcast, upcast Downcast: Base* to Derived* The compiler will tust you can do treat the pointer as a Derived* The program may crash if the pointer was not a Derived* or worse could happen, i.e Undefined Behavior. You could read garbage. Upcast: Derived* to Base* This can happen implicitly, we don't need to explicitly cast it with static_cast Common usage: std::vector<int> data; /** * You gurantee that data will always fit into an int. * If it does not, expect to see integer overflow * This means "I know it can be represented by an int" */ int n = static_cast<int>(data.size()); /** * C-style * This is bad, this may also do a const_cast or reinterpret_cast * This basically means "I don't care, just do it" */ int n = (int)data.size(); for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {}

A dynamic cast is used when you're not sure what the type of the object is and you want to try-cast it. Typically used to downcast in polymorphic hierarchies Returns a nullptr if the cast fails or throws and error if casting a reference. Impacts performance, it checks the vtable. Depending on the polymorphic hierarchy, this could be a lot in high-performance applications. Safest way to cast. Relies on RTTI Run-Time Type Information: In some high-performance fields, RTTI may be disabled to improve performance. Dynamic cast can also cross-cast
Very dangerous way to cast. Basically says, "take these bits and treat them like type x". Pure bit-reinterpretation
Used to add or remove the const qualifier from a variable. Useful when interfacing with older APIs that aren't "const-correct."

Cast Compile-time Runtime static_cast Usually Sometimes, e.g, for std::string, it calls the constructor dynamic_cast No Yes reinterpret_cast Compile-time Runtime behavior may be unsafe const_cast Compile-time No runtime check C-style cast Depends on what it resolves to Depends static_cast may be compile time if the compiler knows exactly how to do the conversion, e.g int -> float, Base* -> Derived*
You may also be interested in Polymorphism through inheritance and how it ties to casting.
Cross-Casting Mechanics: Hands-On Design Patterns with C++, Chapter 1, Page 16 ("Multiple Inheritance"). Fedor G. Pikus.

Compile-time programming in C++ wasn't designed all at once; it evolved from a happy accident into a first-class language feature. Era Key Feature Description C++98 Macros & Template Metaprogramming (TMP) Macros were crude. TMP was discovered by accident—realizing the template system is a Turing-complete language executed at compile time. It used a horrible C++11/14 constexpr The game-changer. Allowed you to write regular-looking C++ functions that could execute at compile time. C++17 if constexpr Allowed compile-time conditional branching C++20 consteval & constinit Introduced immediate functions (guaranteed compile-time) and formal Concepts to replace messy template constraints.
Marcros are a thing of the past now but it is used in many legacy systems and can still have it uses. Marcros are evaluated at compile time based on compiler flags most of the time.

int square(int x) { return x * x; } #define SQUARE(x) square(x) template <typename... Args> void log(Args&&... args) { (std::cout << ... << args); std::cout << std::endl; } #define LOG(...) log(__VA_ARGS__)

This is the most common modern use #define DEBUG 1 int main() { std::cout << "Application started." << std::endl; #if DEBUG std::cout << "[LOG] Debug mode is ON. Internal states verified." << std::endl; #else std::cout << "Running in production mode." << std::endl; #endif return 0; }

Templates are very powerfull and one of my most favourite feature. They give you the ability to write generic, type-safe code that the compiler customizes for you on the fly. The compiler uses that blueprint to generate actual functions or classes only when you request them with a specific data type. This process is called template instantiation.

Function templates are used to define generic arguments or return types of the function. When the compiler sees a template being used with a specific datatype, in this case a float or int, it will generate the actual function and perform it's duty, i.e, validate the datatype and the operations performed on it. Add two numbers template <typename T> T add(T a, T b) { return a + b; } Add n numbers template <typename... T> auto add(T&&... args) { return (... + args); }

Templates aren't just for functions; they allow you to create entire containers or classes that can hold any data type. This is exactly how the C++ Standard Template Library (STL) containers like std::vector or std::list work. // Added ttl as a uint64_t just as an example and made a static assert. template <typename T, uint64_t TTL> class ManagedPtr { static_assert(TTL > 0 && (TTL & (TTL - 1)) == 0, "TTL must be a power of two"); private: T* m_Ptr; TTL ttl; public: template <typename... Args> ManagedPtr(Args&&... args): m_Ptr(new T(std::forward<Args>(args)...)) {} ManagedPtr(const ManagedPtr &other) = delete; ManagedPtr& operator=(const ManagedPtr& other) = delete; ManagedPtr(ManagedPtr&& other) noexcept: m_Ptr(nullptr) { std::swap(m_Ptr, other.m_Ptr); }; ManagedPtr& operator=(ManagedPtr&& other) noexcept { if(this != &other) { delete m_Ptr; m_Ptr = other.m_Ptr; other.m_Ptr = nullptr; } return *this; }; ~ManagedPtr() { delete m_Ptr; } T* get() const { return m_Ptr; } }

Sometimes, a generic blueprint doesn't work for a specific type. For example, comparing two numbers is straightforward, but comparing two char* (C-style strings) compares their memory addresses, not their alphabetical value. Template specialization allows you to write a custom "override" blueprint for a specific type. There are two types of specialization: Partial Specialization Full / Explicit Specialization Full specialization for functions: #include <iostream> #include <cstring> // Generic template template <typename T> bool isEqual(T a, T b) { return a == b; } // Explicit specialization for const char* template <> bool isEqual<const char*>(const char* a, const char* b) { return std::strcmp(a, b) == 0; // Correct way to compare C-strings } int main() { // Uses the specialized template because the arguments are const char* std::cout << isEqual("apple", "apple") << std::endl; // True return 0; } Explicit & Partial Specialization for classes: #include <iostream> // Primary template template <int N, int D> struct Ratio { static const int num = N; static const int den = D; void print() const { std::cout << num << "/" << den << std::endl; } }; // Partial Specialization for divide by zero template <int N> struct Ratio<N, 0> { static const int num = N; static const int den = 1; void print() const { std::cout << "Partial Specialization Activated: " << N << "/0" << std::endl; } }; // Explicit (Full) Specialization for 0 / 0 template <> struct Ratio<0, 0> { static const int num = 0; static const int den = 0; void print() const { std::cout << "Explicit Specialization Activated: 0/0" << std::endl; } }; int main() { // Matches the Primary Template Ratio<3, 4> three_quarters; three_quarters.print(); // Matches the Partial Specialization Ratio<5, 0> invalid_denominator; invalid_denominator.print(); // Matches the Explicit Specialization Ratio<0, 0> indeterminate; indeterminate.print(); return 0; }

Instead of writing out the template <typename T> boilerplate, you can use auto directly in the function signature. The C++20 auto equivalent: auto add(auto a, auto b) { return a + b; } auto add(auto&&... args) { return (... + args); } info Difference between original example and auto There is a subtle difference between the original example and auto. In the original example, we use one template parameter T. This meant that both parameters had to be the same type. In the example with auto, C++20 treats each auto as a seperate parameter. As if we wrote: template<typename T1, typename T2> auto add(T1 a, T2 b) { return a + b; } Note: The return type is auto because T1 and T2 could be different types. int & float, we have to use auto as the return type. We could force b to be the same as a by using decltype(a): auto add(auto a, decltype(a) b) { return a + b; }

Note: Use unique pointers, this is just an example #include <iostream> // A global templated variable. template<typename T> T* g_Ptr = nullptr; int main() { g_Ptr<int> = new int(99); std::cout << *g_Ptr<int>; delete g_Ptr<int>; return 0; }

Without template template parameters, if you wanted to pass a container, your template would look like this: template <typename T, typename Container>. The user would then have to instantiate it like this: Stack<int, std::vector<int>> v;. Redundant repetition of int. #include <iostream> #include <vector> #include <deque> template <typename T, template <typename> class Container> class Stack { private: Container<T> elems; public: void push(const T& elem) { elems.push_back(elem); } void pop() { if (!elems.empty()) elems.pop_back(); } T top() const { return elems.back(); } }; int main() { { // Pass 'int' as the data type, and 'std::vector' as the container template Stack<int, std::vector> stack; stack.push(10); stack.push(20); std::cout << "Vector Stack Top: " << stack.top() << std::endl; } { // Easily swap the underlying storage to a std::deque Stack<std::string, std::deque> stack; stack.push("Hello"); stack.push("World"); std::cout << "Deque Stack Top: " << stack.top() << std::endl; // World } return 0; }

Templates don't compile until they are used so errors won't show up until the templated method is called. In the example below, sum() method is invalid for char* but it isn't compile till the method is called. This is Lazy Template Instantiation. Everything else, like push() is valid and it compiles because it was used. Lazy Instantiation: It rules that if a member function of a template class is never called, it does not exist in the final compiled binary. #include <iostream> template <typename T> class Array { private: T m_Arr[2]; public: void push(T a, T b) { m_Arr[0] = a; m_Arr[1] = b; } void sum() { T result = m_Arr[0] + m_Arr[1]; std::cout << result << std::endl; } }; int main() { Array<int> a; a.push(5,5); a.sum(); char data[2] = {'a', 'b'}; Array<char*> b; b.push(&data[0], &data[1]); // b.sum(); // m_Arr[0] + m_Arr[1] is not valid for char* but until we call sum(), that code is never compiled so no error shows up return 0; }

Source: Back to Basics: Concepts in C++ - Nicolai Josuttis - CppCon 2024 Constraints are compile-time boolean values Constraints can be combined with && You can also use || but note that it may add a significant amount of compile-time Constraints can be used with if constexpr because they are compile-time boolean values

Constraints are a part of the methods signature. This is why two overloads with the same arguments are allowed. template<typename Collection> concept HasPushBack = requires (Collection c, Collection::value_type v) { c.push_back(v); }; template<typename Collection, typename T> requires HasPushBack<Collection> void add(Collection& c, const T& v) { c.push_back(v); } template<typename Collection, typename T> void add(Collection& c, const T& v) { c.insert(v); } std::vector<int> a; std::set<int> b; add(a, 9); add(b, 9); tip Compile-type polymorphism Overload resolution prefers the more specialized function if one matches. This is why add(a, 9) works because an overload exists that requires that constraint HasPushBack<Collection> is met. template<typename Collection> concept HasPushBack = requires (Collection c, Collection::value_type v) { c.push_back(v); }; Two constraints are defined here. Collection c has a method push_back. The method push_back accepts a Collection::value_type v as an argument. We could have defined another constraint for insert but in this example it was not necessary since overload resolution prefers the more specialized function. NOTE: Alternative syntax, you can use a concept as a typename. This is the same as requires HasPushBack<Collection> template<HasPushBack Collection, typename T> void add(Collection& c, const T& v) { c.push_back(v); }

How do we write test cases for concepts? template<typename Collection> concept HasPushBack = requires (Collection c, Collection::value_type v) { c.push_back(v); }; // Asserts true for vectors static_assert(HasPushBack<std::vector<int>>); // Asserts false for sets static_assert(HasPushBack<std::set<int>> == false);

template<typename Collection> concept HasPushBack = requires (Collection c, Collection::value_type v) { c.push_back(v); }; void add(HasPushBack auto& c, const auto& v) { c.push_back(v); } void add(auto& c, const auto& v) { c.insert(v); } std::vector<int> a; std::set<int> b; add(a, 9); add(b, 9); Using auto we can simplify the original templated methods. Notice the usage of the concept here: void add(HasPushBack auto& c, const auto& v) This is how we constraint auto. Alternatively you could also constraint auto like this: template<typename Collection> concept HasPushBack = requires (Collection c, Collection::value_type v) { c.push_back(v); }; // Invalid, can't use decltype(c) here. void add(auto& c, const auto& v) requires HasPushBack<decltype(c)> { c.push_back(v); } The problem here is that auto& c is an L value reference. In the concept we are using Collection::value_type. std::vector<int>&::value_type is invalid because references don't have members Solution: Remove the reference void add(auto& c, const auto& v) requires HasPushBack<std::remove_cvref_t<decltype(c)>> { c.push_back(v); } note std::decay_t<T> vs std::remove_cvref_t<T> You may think that std::decay_t<T> is okay to use instead of std::remove_cvref_t<T> but std::decay_t<T> removes both const, volatile & references. std::decay_t<T> also converts arrays to pointers. This may not be what you want. std::remove_cvref_t<T> removes only the reference. Can we make the concept take care of this so we don't have to have requires HasPushBack<std::remove_cvref_t<decltype(c)>>? We can, here is how: template<typename Collection> concept HasPushBack = requires (Collection c, std::remove_cvref_t<Collection>::value_type v) { c.push_back(v); }; Our code now looks like: template<typename Collection> concept HasPushBack = requires (Collection c, std::remove_cvref_t<Collection>::value_type v) { c.push_back(v); }; // Test case: static_assert(HasPushBack<std::vector<int>&>); // will assert true void add(auto& c, const auto& v) requires HasPushBack<decltype(c)> { c.push_back(v); } void add(auto& c, const auto& v) { c.insert(v); } std::vector<int> a; std::set<int> b; add(a, 9); add(b, 9);

A more simple way to do this is to use multiple parameters in our concept: template<typename Collection, typename T> concept CanPushBack = requires (Collection c, T v) { c.push_back(v); }; void add(auto& c, const auto& v) requires CanPushBack<decltype(c), decltype(v)> { c.push_back(v); } // OR template <typename Collection, typename T> void add(Collection& c, const T& v) requires CanPushBack<Collection, T> { c.push_back(v); }

template <typename Collection> concept SequenceCont = std::ranges::range<Collection> && requires (std::remove_cvref_t<Collection> c, std::ranges::range_value_t<Collection> v) { typename std::remove_cvref_t<Collection>::value_type; // Must have a value_type typename std::remove_cvref_t<Collection>::iterator; // Must have an iterator type typename std::remove_cvref_t<Collection>::const_iterator; // Must have an iterator type c.push_back(v); // Can I push back? c.pop_back(); // Can I pop back? c.insert(c.begin(), v); // Can I insert by passing an iterator and a value? c.erase(c.begin()); // Can I erase by passing an iterator? c.clear(); // Can I clear? std::remove_cvref_t<Collection>{v,v,v}; // Can I call the constructor this way? c = {v,v,v}; // Can I have an assignment? {c < c} -> std::convertible_to<bool>; // Can I compair with less than and does it return a bool? }; std::convertible_to<> & std::ranges::range<> are concepts in the standard library. Here we were able to create a concept that checks if a type satisfies multiple concepts and requirements.
require can be used in two different ways, require expression & require clause
template<typename Collection, typename T> concept CanPushBack = requires (Collection c, T v) { c.push_back(v); };
void add(auto& c, const auto& v) requires CanPushBack<decltype(c), decltype(v)> { c.push_back(v); }
This works but it's better not to use it. requires requires [definition] void add(auto& c, const auto& v) requires requires { c.push_back(v); } { c.push_back(v); }

Constraints can be used with if constexpr because they are compile-time boolean values void add(auto& c, const auto& v) { if constexpr (requires { c.push_back(v); }) { c.push_back(v); } else { c.insert(v); } } note With if constexpr and requires we can do a lot. We can use complex concepts we define like SequenceCont We can chain requires with && since they are compile time booleans

Since both approaches solve the exact same problem, here is a quick guide for your notes on when to use Constrained Overloads vs. Inline if constexpr: Feature Constrained Overloads (requires clause) Inline if constexpr + requires Readability Better for architectural separation. Keeps functions short and single-purpose. Better when the logic is simple and localized inside one function. Error Messages Excellent. If a constraint fails, the compiler tells you exactly which function signature failed. Can sometimes lead to longer error messages inside the function body if a branch fails to compile. Extensibility Open for extension. Other programmers can add new overloads for custom types without touching your original code. Closed for extension. If a new type needs a brand new edge case, you must modify the original function to add another else if constexpr.

documents.t: Hands-On Design Patterns with C++, Chapter 2, ("Class and Function Templates"). Fedor G. Pikus.
There are 3 ways to initialize in C++ Copy initialization Direct initialization List initialization List initialization should always be the prefered method of initializing in C++.

Copy-initialization is performed in the following situations: When a named variable (automatic, static, or thread-local) of a non-reference type T is declared with the initializer consisting of an equals sign followed by an expression. (until C++11) When a named variable of a scalar type T is declared with the initializer consisting of an equals sign followed by a brace-enclosed expression (Note: as of C++11, this is classified as list initialization, and narrowing conversion is not allowed). When passing an argument to a function by value. When returning from a function that returns by value. When throwing or catching an exception by value. As part of aggregate initialization, to initialize each element for which an initializer is provided. 1. Named variable initialized with an equals sign​ This is the most common form of copy-initialization. #include <iostream> class Int { private: int m_value; public: Int(int value): m_value(value) {}; void print() { std::cout << m_value << std::endl; } }; int main() { // Copy initialization Int a = 10; a.print(); return 0; } 2. (Until C++11) Scalar initialized with an equals sign and a brace-enclosed expression​ Before C++11, writing = {expr} for basic scalar types (like int, double, pointer) fell under copy-initialization rules. (In modern C++11 and later, this is routed through list-initialization rules to prevent precision loss/narrowing but it still does a copy so it's copy-list-initialization). Int a = {10}; contains an equals sign, it retains its conceptual "copy" nature. The compiler restricts it from using any constructor marked as explicit Int a{10};, being direct initialization, is fully permitted to use explicit constructors. #include <iostream> class Int { private: int m_value; public: Int(int value): m_value(value) {}; void print() { std::cout << m_value << std::endl; } }; int main() { Int a = {3}; // works // Int a = {3.14}; // ERROR: prevents narrowing because it's list-initialization rules return 0; } tip Try making class Int constructor explicit and run the example. You will have to make the initialization explicit Int a = Int{3}; 3. Passing an argument to a function by value​ When a function expects an object by value, and you pass it a raw value (like an int), the compiler uses copy-initialization to construct the function parameter right on the call stack. #include <iostream> class Int { private: int m_value; public: Int(int value) : m_value(value) {} void print() { std::cout << m_value << std::endl; } }; // Expects an 'Int' object passed by value void display(Int obj) { obj.print(); } int main() { // The compiler copy-initializes 'obj' using Int(100). display(100); return 0; } This behaviour may not be prefered in many cased, if so, make the Int consturctor explicit 4. Returning from a function that returns by value​ When a function returns a type by value, the temporary return object initialized at the return statement is copy-initialized from the expression provided. #include <iostream> class Int { private: int m_value; public: Int(int value) : m_value(value) {} void print() { std::cout << m_value << std::endl; } }; // Returns an 'Int' object by value Int createInt() { // The return value is copy-initialized from the integer 500 return 500; } int main() { Int a = createInt(); a.print(); return 0; } note Modern C++ compilers heavily optimize this specific situation using Named Return Value Optimization (NRVO) to skip the actual copy/move copy operation, but conceptually and syntactically, it remains governed by copy-initialization 5. Throwing or catching an exception by value​ When you throw an exception object, the executable constructs a temporary exception object using copy-initialization. Similarly, if you catch the exception by value (though catching by reference is preferred), it gets copy-initialized again into the catch block. #include <iostream> class IntException { public: int m_code; IntException(int code) : m_code(code) {} }; int main() { try { // The thrown temporary object is copy-initialized from '404' throw 404; } catch (IntException ex) { // 'ex' is copy-initialized from the thrown exception std::cout << "Caught error code: " << ex.m_code << std::endl; } return 0; } 6. As part of aggregate initialization​ An aggregate (like a plain old struct or array) can be initialized with a braced list. For every element you explicitly provide an expression for, that specific element is copy-initialized. #include <iostream> class Int { private: int m_value; public: Int(int value) : m_value(value) {} void print() { std::cout << m_value << std::endl; } }; struct IntCollection { Int first; Int second; }; int main() { // Aggregate initialization. // 'first' is copy-initialized with 1, 'second' is copy-initialized with 2. IntCollection collection = {1, 2}; collection.first.print(); collection.second.print(); return 0; }

A class is built to encapsulate behaviour & state It exposes members through a well thought-out design. To enforce encapulation we make use of access modifiers private: Only this class can access private members. protected: Only this class and derived classes can access these members. public: Members that are public are accessable outside the class.
A class should hide implementation while exposing only members that are required To enforce this, we use abstract classes, interfaces.

These two concepts seem a bit too related so here are some differences Encapulation Abstraction Hides data Hides implementation Focuses on security Focuses on simplicity Uses access modifiers Uses abstract classes/interfaces
Common behaviour can be abstracted & encapsulate into a base class. Other classes can inherite properties & behaviour from a base class. Inheritance promotes code reusability

Polymorphism means many forms. There are two types of polymorphism. Compile time & Runtime polymorphism. Compile-time Polymorphism: Achieved via Method Overloading, Operator Overloading, Curiously Recurring Template Pattern (CRTP), Templates. Runtime Polymorphism: Achieved via Function Overriding using the virtual keyword. This allows a base class pointer to call the specific implementation of a derived class.

Curiously Recurring Template Pattern (CRTP) is a C++ technique where a class inherits from a template instantiated with itself. The derived class passes itself as the template parameter. It is often used to emulate interfaces/mixins without virtual overhead. template<typename Derived> class Animal { public: void speak() { static_cast<Derived*>(this)->speakImplementation(); } }; class Dog : public Animal<Dog> { public: void speakImplementation() { std::cout << "Bark" << std::endl; } }; Note: Important Limitation CRTP is not substitutable polymorphism. You cannot do: Animal* a = new Dog(); // invalid

class Animal { public: virtual ~Animal() {} virtual void speak() { std::cout << "..." << std::endl; } }; class Dog : public Animal { public: void speak() override { std::cout << "Bark" << std::endl; } }; Animal* animal = &baseAnimal; // speak() should output "..." Dog dog; // speak() should output "Bark" animal = &dog; animal->speak(); // "Bark" When you call animal->speak();, the program looks into the vtable (virtual table) for the Dog class and executes Dog::speak() Even though the typeof(animal) is Animal. This is runtime polymorphism.

CRTP Virtual Functions Compile-time dispatch Runtime dispatch No vtable Uses vtable No RTTI required RTTI often associated Can inline easily Harder to inline Faster More flexible Types fixed at compile time Types chosen at runtime

It ties the lifetime of a resource to the lifetime of a stack object. An object should be responsible for any resource it creates. All resources should be aquired in the constructor and released in the destructor

Acquisition: The resource (memory, file handle, mutex lock) is acquired in the constructor. If acquisition fails, the constructor throws an exception, and the object is never considered "alive." Ownership: The object "owns" the resource for its entire lifespan. Release: The resource is automatically released in the destructor. template <typename T> class ManagedPtr { private: T* m_Ptr; public: template <typename... Args> ManagedPtr(Args&&... args): m_Ptr(new T(std::forward<Args>(args)...)) {} ManagedPtr(const ManagedPtr &other) = delete; ManagedPtr& operator=(const ManagedPtr& other) = delete; ManagedPtr(ManagedPtr&& other) noexcept: m_Ptr(nullptr) { std::swap(m_Ptr, other.m_Ptr); }; ManagedPtr& operator=(ManagedPtr&& other) noexcept { if(this != &other) { delete m_Ptr; m_Ptr = other.m_Ptr; other.m_Ptr = nullptr; } return *this; }; ~ManagedPtr() { delete m_Ptr; } T* get() const { return m_Ptr; } } Notice how the Rule of 5 was covered for here. Copy & move constructor and assigment were deleted. note A Note on noexcept You should always mark move constructors and move assignment operators as noexcept. Standard library containers (like std::vector) are designed to be "exception-safe." If a move operation could potentially throw an exception during a resize, the vector will default to a slow copy instead of a move. This ensures that if the operation fails halfway through, the original data remains untouched. Since swapping pointers in an RAII object like ManagedPtr is guaranteed not to throw, marking it noexcept signals the compiler that it's safe to use the high-performance move logic.

A class must not have more than one responsibility. Higher Cohesion: The code inside the class actually belongs together. Lower Coupling: Classes depend on each other less, making it safer to swap parts out. Testability: It’s much easier to write a unit test for a class that only does one thing. The God Object is the anti-pattern that SRP solves class Policy { void apply(); void logoutUser(); // This is bad }

Open for extension, closed for modification A class should not be modified once it has been written. Regression Bugs: Breaking a feature that was working perfectly for months. Testability: You don't have to retest everything since it is closed for modification. Merge Conflicts: Multiple developers trying to edit the same core file for different features. // Closed for modification class IPolicy { virtual ~IPolicy() = default; virtual void apply(const User &user) = 0; } // New features are added here, open for extension class RemoteWorkPolicy : public IPolicy { void apply(const User &user) override { // Business logic... } }; class Attandance { // Open for extension through dependency injection IPolicy* m_Policy; Attendance(IPolicy* policy) : m_Policy(policy) {} void markAttandance(const User &user) { if(!policy) throw std::runtime_exception("Unexpected"); policy.apply(user); } }

If (S) is a subtype of (T), then objects of type (T) should be replaceable with objects of type (S) without breaking the application. The Square-Rectangle problem: If a Square inherits from Rectangle but throws an error when you try to set the width and height to different values, it violates LSP. Code expecting a Rectangle might behave incorrectly when passed a Square. Principle of Least Astonishment: If a function takes an IPolicy*, it should be able to use any subclass without needing to check which one it is or handling unexpected "Not Implemented" errors.

class IPolicy { public: virtual ~IPolicy() = default; virtual void apply(const User& user) = 0; }; // It only works for Admin users, otherwise it crashes/throws. class AdminOnlyPolicy : public IPolicy { public: void apply(const User& user) override { if (!user.isAdmin()) { // This violates LSP because the caller of IPolicy // doesn't expect apply() to fail based on user type. throw std::runtime_error("LSP Broken: Unexpected user type!"); } // Business logic... } }; The base contract: virtual void apply(const User& user) = 0; implies: Any User is valid input. Callers should be able to pass any User to any IPolicy. But AdminOnlyPolicy secretly strengthens the precondition: if (!user.isAdmin()) Now the function only accepts a subset of User. That means code like this becomes unsafe: void runPolicy(IPolicy& policy, const User& user) { policy.apply(user); }

One possible solution to this problem class IPolicy { public: virtual ~IPolicy() = default; // We define a new method, it returns false if it's not applicable virtual bool canApply(const User& user) const = 0; virtual void apply(const User& user) = 0; }; class AdminOnlyPolicy : public IPolicy { public: bool canApply(const User& user) const override { return !user.isAdmin(); // Explicitly states its limits } void apply(const User& user) override { // We know this is safe because the caller checks canApply first user.setPermissions(LOW); } }; // The consumer can now use any IPolicy safely void processAttendance(User& user, IPolicy* policy) { if (policy->canApply(user)) policy->apply(user); // No surprises here } Make rejection part of the base contract: virtual bool canApply(const User& user) const = 0; Then callers know rejection is normal behavior rather than a broken substitution.

No client should be forced to depend on methods it does not use. Splitting the "Fat Interface": Instead of one massive IPolicy that handles attendance, payroll, and security, you split them into smaller, focused interfaces. This prevents "Side-Effect Recompilation." If you change a payroll method in a giant interface, your attendance module (which doesn't care about payroll) still has to recompile. // A "Fat" Interface class IEmployeeActions { virtual void calculatePay() = 0; // for Accountant virtual void writeCode() = 0; // for Developer - Why does the Accountant need this? }; // Segregated Interfaces class IDeveloper { virtual void writeCode() = 0; }; class IAccountant { virtual void calculatePay() = 0; };

High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on abstractions. Attendance class (high-level) shouldn't know about WiFiPolicy or FingerprintPolicy (low-level details). It only knows about IPolicy (the abstraction). This is the ultimate goal of the previous four principles. It allows you to swap out a database, a UI framework, or a business rule without touching the core orchestration logic. Attendance depends on the abstraction IPolicy. It does not depend on any concrete class class IPolicy { virtual ~IPolicy() = default; virtual void apply(const User &user) = 0; } class Attandance { IPolicy* m_Policy; Attendance(IPolicy* policy) : m_Policy(policy) {} }

Object Name Acronym Purpose Vertex Array Object VAO Stores the links between VBO/EBO bindings and Shader attribute configurations. Acts as the execution blueprint wrapper for a mesh. Vertex Buffer Object VBO A generic memory block target containing raw array data (e.g., Positions, Normals, Colors, UVs) sent to the vertex shader. Element Buffer Object EBO / IBO Stores index maps determining the sequence in which vertices are assembled into geometric primitives (Triangles, Lines). Framebuffer Object FBO A user-defined, off-screen rendering target wrapper that allows you to render your scene directly into textures instead of the screen display. Renderbuffer Object RBO An optimized, write-only data image storage buffer. Used inside FBOs for high-speed hardware tasks like Depth and Stencil testing. Texture Object TEX Manages 1D, 2D, 3D, or Cube-map image data arrays along with sampling states (Filtering, Mipmapping, Texturing wrap settings). Shader Object Shader Holds a single compiled stage of GLSL source binary (e.g., Vertex, Fragment, Geometry, Tessellation, or Compute). Program Object Program A collection of multiple attached Shader Objects linked together to form a complete execution pipeline handle (glUseProgram). Program Pipeline Object PPO Allows mixing and matching individual separate shader stages from completely different programs on the fly without a full re-link penalty. Transform Feedback Object TFO Records the raw vertex data streams shooting out of the geometry pipeline backend before they ever hit the rasterizer. Sampler Object Sampler Separates texturing filtering states (Linear, Nearest, Repeat) from the underlying texture image allocation data itself. Query Object Query Measures asynchronous hardware execution milestones (e.g., GPU time elapsed, primitive occlusion count, fragments passed). Sync Object Sync / Fence Creates an execution fence sync token to synchronize commands between the asynchronous CPU driver thread and the GPU processing cores. Pixel Buffer Object PBO Enables asynchronous, non-blocking texture uploads and screen read-backs using background DMA channels. Texture Buffer Object TBO Allows shaders to treat a massive, flat memory buffer as a 1D texture array bypass limit restrictions.

Purpose: Vertex attributes (Positions, UVs, Normals, Colors). Why use it: This is the baseline entry point for geometry. You use it to stream raw vertex data layout arrays from the CPU into the GPU's high-speed VRAM so the vertex shader can process them. Example: Standard | drawn via glDrawArrays

Purpose: Vertex array indices. Why use it: Indexed rendering. Instead of duplicating vertex data for shared corners (like a cube where vertices meet), you list unique vertices once in an array buffer and use this target to define the triangle construction sequence using lightweight integer lookups. Example: Standard | drawn via glDrawElements

Purpose: Uniform block storage. Why use it: Performance scaling for shared global values. If you have 50 different shader programs that all need access to the same View and Projection matrices, you upload them once to a UBO instead of executing 50 individual CPU-stalling glUniformMatrix4fv calls. GLuint ubo; glGenBuffers(1, &ubo); glBindBuffer(GL_UNIFORM_BUFFER, ubo); glBufferData(GL_UNIFORM_BUFFER, sizeof(glm::mat4) * 2, nullptr, GL_STREAM_DRAW); // binding = 0 glBindBufferBase(GL_UNIFORM_BUFFER, 0, ubo); // Update data per frame (GL_STREAM_DRAW) glBufferSubData(GL_UNIFORM_BUFFER, 0, sizeof(glm::mat4), &viewMatrix[0][0]); layout (std140, binding = 0) uniform Scene { mat4 view; mat4 projection; }; note glBufferSubData & per frame updates High speed memory: When you initially allocate your buffer, use GL_STREAM_DRAW instead of GL_STATIC_DRAW or GL_DYNAMIC_DRAW. This acts as a hint to the driver that the data will be modified frequently and read immediately. Batch data: Calling glBufferSubData once for one giant chunk of data is much faster than calling it hundreds of times for tiny individual objects in a single frame. For best performance, consider using Persistent Mapped Buffers (GL_MAP_PERSISTENT_BIT) introduced in OpenGL 4.4

Purpose: Read-write storage for shaders. Why use it: Massive capacity data streaming with write-back capabilities. Unlike UBOs (which are small and read-only), SSBOs can handle gigabytes of data and allow shaders to dynamically modify their contents. Perfect for large-scale particle physical systems or passing dynamically calculated light lists to a forward+ renderer. GLuint ssbo; glGenBuffers(1, &ssbo); glBindBuffer(GL_SHADER_STORAGE_BUFFER, ssbo); glBufferData(GL_SHADER_STORAGE_BUFFER, sizeof(Particle) * 10000, nullptr, GL_DYNAMIC_COPY); // binding = 1 glBindBufferBase(GL_SHADER_STORAGE_BUFFER, 1, ssbo); struct Particle { vec4 position; vec4 velocity; }; layout (std430, binding = 1) buffer Particles { Particle particles[]; };

Purpose: Indirect draw command arguments. Why use it: GPU-Driven Rendering. Instead of the CPU tracking what to draw every frame, the CPU tells the GPU once: "Read your draw parameters from this memory block." The GPU (via compute shaders) calculates object visibility, performs culling, and writes the final visible primitive counts straight into this buffer without talking back to the CPU. NOTE: The example below is cpu-driven, to make it gpu-driven I would need to create a compute shader, bind the dib (indirect draw command) to the compute shader, in the shader I would toggle which draw command to run by setting instanceCount to 0 or 1/2/3/... struct DrawElementsIndirectCommand { GLuint count; // Element/Index Count GLuint instanceCount; // Instances to duplicate GLuint firstIndex; // EBO Offset GLint baseVertex; // VBO Offset GLuint baseInstance; // Instancing Offset }; std::vector<DrawElementsIndirectCommand> commands; GLuint dib; glGenBuffers(1, &dib); glBindBuffer(GL_DRAW_INDIRECT_BUFFER, dib); glBufferData(GL_DRAW_INDIRECT_BUFFER, commands.size() * sizeof(DrawElementsIndirectCommand), commands.data(), GL_DYNAMIC_DRAW); // Execute one (command at index 2) glDrawElementsIndirect(GL_TRIANGLES, GL_UNSIGNED_INT, (void*)(sizeof(DrawElementsIndirectCommand) * 2)); // Execute many (from index 0 till the end) glMultiDrawElementsIndirect(GL_TRIANGLES, GL_UNSIGNED_INT, (void*)0, commands.size(), 0);

Purpose: Indirect compute dispatch commands. Why use it: The exact compute shader equivalent of GL_DRAW_INDIRECT_BUFFER. If you are running a chain of compute operations (e.g., fluid particle simulation where a variable number of items pass criteria checks), the first compute shader can write the exact workgroup configuration dimensions (X,Y,Z)(X, Y, Z)(X,Y,Z) into this buffer for the subsequent compute pass to fetch. struct DispatchCommand { GLuint groups_x; GLuint groups_y; GLuint groups_z; }; DispatchCommand command{128,1,1}; GLuint buffer; glGenBuffers(1, &buffer); glBindBuffer(GL_DISPATCH_INDIRECT_BUFFER, buffer); glBufferData(GL_DISPATCH_INDIRECT_BUFFER, sizeof(DispatchCommand), &command, GL_DYNAMIC_DRAW); // Executes computation directly from GPU-calculated sizing blocks glDispatchComputeIndirect(0); GL_DISPATCH_INDIRECT_BUFFER is used less often in beginner projects so here is an explaination.​ GL_DISPATCH_INDIRECT_BUFFER is the compute-shader equivalent of indirect drawing. Normally, when you launch a compute shader, the CPU decides how many workgroups to run: glDispatchCompute(128, 1, 1); Meaning: Run: 128 workgroups in X 1 workgroup in Y 1 workgroup in Z We chose those number on the CPU side. With indirect dispatch glDispatchComputeIndirect(0); the GPU reads the workgroup counts from a buffer instead. That buffer is bound to GL_DISPATCH_INDIRECT_BUFFER OpenGL expects exactly this layout: struct DispatchCommand { GLuint num_groups_x; GLuint num_groups_y; GLuint num_groups_z; }; DispatchCommand command{128,1,1}; /// glGenBuffers, glBindBuffer & glBufferData with that command glBufferData(GL_DISPATCH_INDIRECT_BUFFER, sizeof(DispatchCommand), &command, GL_DYNAMIC_DRAW); // Dispatch a compute shader glDispatchComputeIndirect(0); Equivalent to glDispatchCompute(128, 1, 1); You may think that this is still cpu-drivin and you would be correct. What makes this special is that the DispatchCommand can be bound to another compute shader and be modified. Example pipeline: Compute Pass A: Count visible particles atomicCounterIncrement(visibleCount) ↓ Writes: num_groups_x = visibleCount / 64 ↓ Compute Pass B: Uses glDispatchComputeIndirect() ↓ GPU decides its own workload size Now the CPU never calculates dispatch sizes.

Purpose: Atomic counter storage. Why use it: Why use any atomics? The same reason applies except this is exclusively for optimized for increment/decrement patterns. To avoid data races and have a lock free program. You could imagine, we would use GL_ATOMIC_COUNTER_BUFFER in the above example pipeline, Compute Pass A is a compute shader that needs to count visible particles. We have to use an atomic counter here. visibleCount in the example above is atomic. GLuint initialValue = 0; GLuint atomicCounter; glGenBuffers(1, &atomicCounter); glBindBuffer(GL_ATOMIC_COUNTER_BUFFER, atomicCounter); glBufferData(GL_ATOMIC_COUNTER_BUFFER, sizeof(GLuint), &initialValue, GL_DYNAMIC_COPY); // binding = 0 glBindBufferBase(GL_ATOMIC_COUNTER_BUFFER, 0, atomicCounter); layout (binding = 0, offset = 0) uniform atomic_uint visibleCount; void main() { if(visible) atomicCounterIncrement(visibleCount); } info glBindBufferBase is used to bind buffers to slots in the shader glBindBufferBase(GL_ATOMIC_COUNTER_BUFFER, 0, atomicCounter); Bind buffer "atomicCounter" to atomic counter binding slot 0, in the shader: layout(binding = 0, offset = 0) uniform atomic_uint visibleCount; glBindBuffer is only for cpu access, if you want to glBufferData or glMapBuffer (updating buffer data) glBindBufferBase is for gpu access, if you want to bind something to a slot. Refer this

Legacy feature superseded by Compute Shaders Purpose: Transform feedback buffer capture. Why use it: Bypassing pixel generation. Used when you want to use vertex, tessellation, or geometry shaders strictly as a data processing engine (e.g., simulating gravitational physics or cloth vertices). It catches the transformed vertices shooting out of the geometry backend and writes them directly back into a standard VBO, omitting the fragment shader. GLuint tfb; glGenBuffers(1, &tfb); glBindBuffer(GL_TRANSFORM_FEEDBACK_BUFFER, tfb); glBufferData(GL_TRANSFORM_FEEDBACK_BUFFER, sizeof(vertices), nullptr, GL_DYNAMIC_COPY); glBindBufferBase(GL_TRANSFORM_FEEDBACK_BUFFER, 0, tfb); // Capturing state execution sequence glBeginTransformFeedback(GL_POINTS); glDrawArrays(GL_POINTS, 0, 100); glEndTransformFeedback();

Purpose: Texture data buffer (TBO). Why use it: Massive flat textures. Standard 2D textures have dimensions bounds (e.g., 16384x16384). If you need to map a huge, arbitrary structural table array to your texture shaders, you allocate a standard flat data buffer and use a TBO sampler wrapper inside your shader to access it linearly like a giant array lookup. struct LightData { float position[4]; float color[4]; }; LightData lights[] = { { { 0.0f, 5.0f, 0.0f, 1.0f }, { 1.0f, 0.8f, 0.6f, 1.0f } }, { { 3.0f, 2.0f, 1.0f, 1.0f }, { 0.2f, 0.5f, 1.0f, 1.0f } } }; GLuint tbo, tboTex; glGenBuffers(1, &tbo); glBindBuffer(GL_TEXTURE_BUFFER, tbo); glBufferData(GL_TEXTURE_BUFFER, sizeof(lights), lights, GL_STATIC_DRAW); glGenTextures(1, &tboTex); glBindTexture(GL_TEXTURE_BUFFER, tboTex); // Note: GL_RGBA32F because of the data glTexBuffer(GL_TEXTURE_BUFFER, GL_RGBA32F, tbo); // Link buffer data array layout to texture wrapper uniform samplerBuffer lightBuffer; struct Light { vec4 position; vec4 color; }; void main() { uint lightIndex = 1; Light light; light.position = texelFetch(lightBuffer, lightIndex * 2 + 0); light.color = texelFetch(lightBuffer, lightIndex * 2 + 1); vec3 color = light.color.rgb; } Texture buffers are often used for: Large material tables Animation matrices Skinning data Light lists Terrain metadata Particle attributes GPU lookup tables

Purpose: Agnostic targets for memory block duplication. Why use it: Asynchronous memory transfers. When copying data inside memory blocks directly on the GPU (e.g., copying vertex physics calculations into a rendering mesh VBO), you bind the source to COPY_READ and destination to COPY_WRITE. This prevents binding state contamination on your primary asset arrays (GL_ARRAY_BUFFER). glBindBuffer(GL_COPY_READ_BUFFER, sourceVBO); glBindBuffer(GL_COPY_WRITE_BUFFER, destinationVBO); // Copy 1024 bytes directly across hardware lanes without CPU stalls glCopyBufferSubData(GL_COPY_READ_BUFFER, GL_COPY_WRITE_BUFFER, 0, 0, 1024);

Purpose: PBO operations (Packing = Read from GPU / Unpacking = Write to GPU). Why use it: Asynchronous texture mapping and screenshot streaming. Normally, calling glTexImage2D forces the CPU to sit still while data uploads to VRAM. By mapping a PBO as an intermediate waystation, you upload data to the PBO asynchronously, and the subsequent texture call runs instantaneously at pure DMA (Direct Memory Access) speeds. GLuint pbo; glGenBuffers(1, &pbo); glBindBuffer(GL_PIXEL_UNPACK_BUFFER, pbo); glBufferData(GL_PIXEL_UNPACK_BUFFER, width * height * 4, nullptr, GL_STREAM_DRAW); // Map pointer to write directly from CPU image library loaders void* ptr = glMapBuffer(GL_PIXEL_UNPACK_BUFFER, GL_WRITE_ONLY); memcpy(ptr, imageData, width * height * 4); glUnmapBuffer(GL_PIXEL_UNPACK_BUFFER); glBindTexture(GL_TEXTURE_2D, textureID); // This call completes instantly because data transfers directly from PBO memory! glTexImage2D(GL_TEXTURE_2D, 0, GL_RGBA, width, height, 0, GL_RGBA, GL_UNSIGNED_BYTE, nullptr);

Purpose: Asynchronous hardware profiling and query evaluation. Why use it: Stopping CPU pipeline stalls during performance tracking. Normally, if you ask OpenGL for performance timing or occlusion counts (glGetQueryObjectuiv), the CPU must freeze and wait for the GPU to catch up. By binding a GL_QUERY_BUFFER, the query results are written straight into a GPU memory address asynchronously, letting your frame logic proceed undisturbed. GLuint qbo; glGenBuffers(1, &qbo); glBindBuffer(GL_QUERY_BUFFER, qbo); glBufferData(GL_QUERY_BUFFER, sizeof(GLuint), nullptr, GL_DYNAMIC_COPY); // Capture execution pipeline statistics glEndQuery(GL_TIME_ELAPSED); // Tell driver to copy query results directly into buffer memory layout block instead of returning to variable glGetQueryObjectuiv(queryID, GL_QUERY_RESULT, (GLuint*)0);

glEnable(GL_DEPTH_TEST); // Enable depth testing (prevents distant fragments overlapping close ones) glEnable(GL_BLEND); // Enable alpha blending (Transparency) glBlendFunc(GL_SRC_ALPHA, GL_ONE_MINUS_SRC_ALPHA); // Default blending function glClearColor(0.1f, 0.2f, 0.3f, 1.0f); // Define background clear color glViewport(0, 0, width, height); // Map normalized device coordinates to screen pixels

GLuint VBO; glGenBuffers(1, &VBO); glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, VBO); glBufferData(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, sizeof(data), data, GL_STATIC_DRAW); // GL_STATIC_DRAW, GL_DYNAMIC_DRAW, GL_STREAM_DRAW glDeleteBuffers(1, &VBO); info GL_STATIC_DRAW: I won't change the buffer data GL_DYNAMIC_DRAW: I may change the buffer data it once in a while GL_STREAM_DRAW: I will change the buffer data it every frame

// Set a 4x4 matrix uniform in shader GLint matLoc = glGetUniformLocation(shaderProgramID, "u_ModelMatrix"); glUniformMatrix4fv(matLoc, 1, GL_FALSE, glm::value_ptr(modelMatrix)); // Set an integer / texture sampler unit GLint texLoc = glGetUniformLocation(shaderProgramID, "u_Texture"); glUniform1i(texLoc, 0); // Binds sampler to Texture Unit 0

#include <iostream> #include <string> #include <vector> #include <GL/glew.h> /** * @brief Compiles a single shader stage from source code string. * @param shaderType GL_VERTEX_SHADER | GL_FRAGMENT_SHADER | GL_GEOMETRY_SHADER | GL_TESS_CONTROL_SHADER | GL_TESS_EVALUATION_SHADER | GL_COMPUTE_SHADER * @param shaderSourceCode The raw GLSL source code string. * @return GLuint The compiled shader ID, or 0 if compilation failed. */ GLuint compileShaderStage(GLenum shaderType, const std::string& shaderSourceCode) { std::string stageName = "UNKNOWN"; switch (shaderType) { case GL_VERTEX_SHADER: stageName = "VERTEX"; break; case GL_TESS_CONTROL_SHADER: stageName = "TESS_CONTROL"; break; case GL_TESS_EVALUATION_SHADER: stageName = "TESS_EVALUATION"; break; case GL_GEOMETRY_SHADER: stageName = "GEOMETRY"; break; case GL_FRAGMENT_SHADER: stageName = "FRAGMENT"; break; case GL_COMPUTE_SHADER: stageName = "COMPUTE"; break; } // Create and source the shader GLuint shader = glCreateShader(shaderType); const char* sourceCStr = shaderSourceCode.c_str(); glShaderSource(shader, 1, &sourceCStr, nullptr); // Compile glCompileShader(shader); // Check for compilation errors GLint success; glGetShaderiv(shader, GL_COMPILE_STATUS, &success); if (!success) { GLint logLength = 0; glGetShaderiv(shader, GL_INFO_LOG_LENGTH, &logLength); std::vector<char> infoLog(logLength); glGetShaderInfoLog(shader, logLength, nullptr, infoLog.data()); std::cerr << "ERROR::SHADER::" << stageName << "::COMPILATION_FAILED
" << infoLog.data() << "
-----------------------------------------------------
" << std::endl; glDeleteShader(shader); return 0; } return shader; } /** * @brief Links multiple shader stages into a unified shader program. * @param shaderStages A vector containing compiled shader handles * @return GLuint The linked shader program ID, or 0 if linking failed. */ GLuint linkShaderProgram(const std::vector<GLuint>& shaderStages) { GLuint program = glCreateProgram(); for (GLuint stage : shaderStages) if (stage != 0) glAttachShader(program, stage); glLinkProgram(program); GLint success; glGetProgramiv(program, GL_LINK_STATUS, &success); if (!success) { GLint logLength = 0; glGetProgramiv(program, GL_INFO_LOG_LENGTH, &logLength); if (logLength > 0) { std::vector<char> infoLog(logLength); glGetProgramInfoLog(program, logLength, nullptr, infoLog.data()); std::cerr << "ERROR::SHADER::PROGRAM::LINKING_FAILED
" << infoLog.data() << "
-----------------------------------------------------
" << std::endl; } else { std::cerr << "ERROR::SHADER::PROGRAM::LINKING_FAILED (Unknown Error)
" << std::endl; } glDeleteProgram(program); return 0; } return program; } int main() { GLuint vs = compileShaderStage(GL_VERTEX_SHADER, vsSource); GLuint tcs = compileShaderStage(GL_TESS_CONTROL_SHADER, tcsSource); GLuint tes = compileShaderStage(GL_TESS_EVALUATION_SHADER, tesSource); GLuint gs = compileShaderStage(GL_GEOMETRY_SHADER, gsSource); GLuint fs = compileShaderStage(GL_FRAGMENT_SHADER, fsSource); GLuint cs = compileShaderStage(GL_COMPUTE_SHADER, csSource); GLuint program = linkShaderProgram({ vs, tcs, tes, gs, fs, cs }); // FYI, this is just an example, cs shaders are generally in a seperate pipeline. // Gotta free memory, don't forget glDeleteShader(vs); glDeleteShader(tcs); glDeleteShader(tes); glDeleteShader(gs); glDeleteShader(fs); glDeleteShader(cs); // eventually glUseProgram(program); }

std::vector<GLfloat> vertices = {0.5f, 0.5f, 0.0f, 0.5f, -0.5f, 0.0f, -0.5f, -0.5f, 0.0f, -0.5f, 0.5f, 0.0f}; std::vector<GLuint> indices = { 0, 1, 3, 1, 2, 3}; GLuint VAO, VBO, EBO; glGenVertexArrays(1, &VAO); glGenBuffers(1, &VBO); glGenBuffers(1, &EBO); glBindVertexArray(VAO); glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, VBO); glBufferData(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, vertices.size() * sizeof(GLfloat), vertices.data(), GL_STATIC_DRAW); glBindBuffer(GL_ELEMENT_ARRAY_BUFFER, EBO); glBufferData(GL_ELEMENT_ARRAY_BUFFER, indices.size() * sizeof(GLuint), indices.data(), GL_STATIC_DRAW); // NOTE: Stride is 5 * sizeof(GLfloat) | [x,y,x,u,v] // Location = 0 // First 3 floats are x,y,z glVertexAttribPointer(0, 3, GL_FLOAT, GL_FALSE, 5 * sizeof(GLfloat), (void*)0); glEnableVertexAttribArray(0); // Location = 1 // Following 2 floats are texture coords (UV) glVertexAttribPointer(1, 2, GL_FLOAT, GL_FALSE, 5 * sizeof(GLfloat), (void*)(3 * sizeof(GLfloat))); glEnableVertexAttribArray(1); // Unbind VAO & VBO // DO NOT UNBIND THE EBO, nothing will render :P // You can unbind the EBO after ending the recording though. But best practice is to never unbind the EBO, doing that is redundant, simply modifies OpenGL state. glBindVertexArray(0); glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, 0); info Multiple VBO one VAO & Multiple EBO one VAO Multiple VBO one VAO: You can use multiple VBOs while recording a VAO. This is actually a nice practice. Location = 0 VBO_postions for vertex data Location = 1 VBO_textures for texture info Location = 2 VBO_normals for normals Multiple EBO one VAO: This is NOT POSSIBLE You can only bind one EBO while recording a VAO Example: // Separate raw data streams std::vector<GLfloat> positions = {}; std::vector<GLfloat> texCoords = {}; std::vector<GLfloat> normals = {}; std::vector<GLuint> indices = {}; // Allocate the Object Handles GLuint VAO; GLuint VBO_Positions, VBO_TexCoords, VBO_Normals; GLuint EBO; glGenVertexArrays(1, &VAO); glGenBuffers(1, &VBO_Positions); glGenBuffers(1, &VBO_TexCoords); glGenBuffers(1, &VBO_Normals); glGenBuffers(1, &EBO); // Start recording glBindVertexArray(VAO); // [VBO 1] Setup Positions & link to layout location = 0 glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, VBO_Positions); glBufferData(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, positions.size() * sizeof(GLfloat), positions.data(), GL_STATIC_DRAW); glVertexAttribPointer(0, 3, GL_FLOAT, GL_FALSE, 3 * sizeof(GLfloat), (void*)0); glEnableVertexAttribArray(0); // [VBO 2] Setup Textures & link to layout location = 1 glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, VBO_TexCoords); glBufferData(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, texCoords.size() * sizeof(GLfloat), texCoords.data(), GL_STATIC_DRAW); glVertexAttribPointer(1, 2, GL_FLOAT, GL_FALSE, 2 * sizeof(GLfloat), (void*)0); // Size is 2 for U,V! glEnableVertexAttribArray(1); // [VBO 3] Setup Normals & link to layout location = 2 glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, VBO_Normals); glBufferData(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, normals.size() * sizeof(GLfloat), normals.data(), GL_STATIC_DRAW); glVertexAttribPointer(2, 3, GL_FLOAT, GL_FALSE, 3 * sizeof(GLfloat), (void*)0); glEnableVertexAttribArray(2); // [EBO] Single shared index buffer glBindBuffer(GL_ELEMENT_ARRAY_BUFFER, EBO); glBufferData(GL_ELEMENT_ARRAY_BUFFER, indices.size() * sizeof(GLuint), indices.data(), GL_STATIC_DRAW); // Stop recording glBindVertexArray(0); glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, 0);

#include <stb_image.h> GLuint textureID; glGenTextures(1, &textureID); glBindTexture(GL_TEXTURE_2D, textureID); // Texture options, there are many, look it up glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_WRAP_S, GL_REPEAT); glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_WRAP_T, GL_REPEAT); glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_MIN_FILTER, GL_LINEAR_MIPMAP_LINEAR); glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_MAG_FILTER, GL_LINEAR); // Load image data int width, height, nrChannels; unsigned char *data = stbi_load("texture.jpg", &width, &height, &nrChannels, 0); if (data) { glTexImage2D(GL_TEXTURE_2D, 0, GL_RGB, width, height, 0, GL_RGB, GL_UNSIGNED_BYTE, data); glGenerateMipmap(GL_TEXTURE_2D); } else { std::cout << "Failed to load texture" << std::endl; } stbi_image_free(data); // Later in the render part glActiveTexture(GL_TEXTURE0); glBindTexture(GL_TEXTURE_2D, textureID);
Always remember to free resources glDeleteVertexArrays(1, &VAO); glDeleteBuffers(1, &VBO); glDeleteBuffers(1, &EBO); glDeleteProgram(shaderProgram); // ...
Local space World space View space Clip space Screen space

Local space is also sometimes called Model space or Object Space Local space is the coordinate system relative to an object's own origin, usually at (0,0,0). The cube (or model) vertices range from (-1,-1,-1) to (1,1,1). It's origin is at (0,0,0)

Local space to World space uses the Model Matrix. vworld=Mmodel⋅vlocal\mathbf{v}_{world} = M_{model} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{local}vworld​=Mmodel​⋅vlocal​ World space to Local space uses the Inverse Model Matrix. vlocal=Mmodel−1⋅vworld\mathbf{v}_{local}= M_{model}^{-1} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{world}vlocal​=Mmodel−1​⋅vworld​ How to calculate Mmodel−1M_{model}^{-1}Mmodel−1​: An model matrix is composed of Translation (TTT), Rotation (RRR), and Scale (SSS): Mmodel=T⋅R⋅SM_{model} = T \cdot R \cdot SMmodel​=T⋅R⋅S To invert it analytically without doing an expensive full matrix inversion calculation, you reverse the operations and invert each individual property: Mmodel−1=S−1⋅R−1⋅T−1M_{model}^{-1} = S^{-1} \cdot R^{-1} \cdot T^{-1}Mmodel−1​=S−1⋅R−1⋅T−1 T−1T^{-1}T−1 (Inverse Translation): Negate the translation values (move backward instead of forward). R−1R^{-1}R−1 (Inverse Rotation): Transpose the rotation matrix (since pure rotation matrices are orthogonal, their transpose equals their inverse), or invert the quaternion. S−1S^{-1}S−1 (Inverse Scale): Reciprocate the scale factors (divide by the scale instead of multiplying, e.g., 1/scalex1/scale_x1/scalex​).
World space places all objects into a common coordinate system. All objects relative to world origin. Cube local vertex = (1,1,1) Cube position in world = (5,0,0) Cube world space = (6,1,1)

World space to View space uses the View Matrix. vview=Mview⋅vworld\mathbf{v}_{view} = M_{view} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{world}vview​=Mview​⋅vworld​ View space to World space uses the Inverse View Matrix. vworld=Mview−1⋅vview\mathbf{v}_{world} = M_{view}^{-1} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{view}vworld​=Mview−1​⋅vview​

In view space, coordinates are relative to the camera. The camera is treated as being at: (0,0,0) looking along a standard direction (often −Z in OpenGL-style conventions). World: Camera = (0,0,10) Object = (0,0,0) View space: Camera = (0,0,0) Object = (0,0,-10) Everything is transformed so the camera becomes the origin.

View space to Clip space uses the Projection Matrix. vclip=Mproj⋅vview\mathbf{v}_{clip} = M_{proj} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{view}vclip​=Mproj​⋅vview​ Clip space to View space uses the Inverse Projection Matrix. vview=Mproj−1⋅vclip\mathbf{v}_{view} = M_{proj}^{-1} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{clip}vview​=Mproj−1​⋅vclip​

A specialized space where coordinates are evaluated to see if they are visible on screen. If a vertex lies outside the boundaries defined by its www component (i.e., −w≤x,y,z≤w-w \le x, y, z \le w−w≤x,y,z≤w), it is "clipped" (discarded).

Clip space to NDC (Normalized Device Coordinates) Perspective Divide. vndc=[x/wy/wz/w]\mathbf{v}_{ndc} = \begin{bmatrix} x/w \\ y/w \\ z/w \end{bmatrix}vndc​=​x/wy/wz/w​​ NDC to Clip space Perspective Multiply. vclip=[nx⋅wny⋅wnz⋅w]\mathbf{v}_{clip} = \begin{bmatrix} nx \cdot w \\ ny \cdot w \\ nz \cdot w \end{bmatrix}vclip​=​nx⋅wny⋅wnz⋅w​​ You've done this or copied it a million times: vec4 clipPosition = projection * view * model * vec4(vPosition, 1.0); vec3 ndc = clipPosition.xyz / clipPosition.w;

The actual pixel coordinates of your monitor/window (e.g., from 0 to 1920 on the X-axis, and 0 to 1080 on the Y-axis). Viewport transform is implemented as a scale-and-bias shift to map the [−1,1][-1, 1][−1,1] NDC cube to [0,width][0, \text{width}][0,width] and [0,height][0, \text{height}][0,height]

Step Operation Matrix / Equation Resulting Space Start Mesh Data Initial Vertex Local Space 1 Apply Model Matrix vw=Mmodel⋅vl\mathbf{v}_{w} = M_{model} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{l}vw​=Mmodel​⋅vl​​ World Space 2 Apply View Matrix vv=Mview⋅vw\mathbf{v}_{v} = M_{view} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{w}vv​=Mview​⋅vw​​ View Space 3 Apply Projection Matrix vc=Mproj⋅vv\mathbf{v}_{c} = M_{proj} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{v}vc​=Mproj​⋅vv​​ Clip Space 4 Perspective Divide (GPU) vndc=vc.xyz/vc.w\mathbf{v}_{ndc} = \mathbf{v}_{c}.xyz / \mathbf{v}_{c}.wvndc​=vc​.xyz/vc​.w NDC Space 5 Viewport Transform (GPU) vs=Viewport(vndc)\mathbf{v}_{s} = \text{Viewport}(\mathbf{v}_{ndc})vs​=Viewport(vndc​) Screen Space info Basically the backward pipeline is the same but with the inverse matrix. For NDC it's multiple by w instead of divide (if you have w saved somewhere). For the Viewport to NDC step, you apply the inverse scale-and-bias of the viewport
Depth testing (the Depth Buffer) is used to prevent triangles rendering in the front while they're supposed to be behind other triangles

The depth buffer is a non-visible 2D buffer that matches the width and height of your screen window. Instead of storing color data like the color buffer, each pixel in the depth buffer stores a depth value (usually a float between 0.0 and 1.0). 0.0 represents the near plane. 1.0 represents the far plane. Depth testing is done in screen space after the fragment shader has run (and after the stencil test). The screen space coordinates relate directly to the viewport defined by OpenGL's glViewport function and can be accessed via GLSL's built-in gl_FragCoord variable in the fragment shader. The x and y components of gl_FragCoord represent the fragment's screen-space coordinates (with (0,0) being the bottom-left corner). The gl_FragCoord variable also contains a z-component which contains the depth value of the fragment. This z value is the value that is compared to the depth buffer's content. note The depth buffer's values are non-linear. note Today most GPUs support a hardware feature called early depth testing. Early depth testing is done after the rasterization stage, before the fragment shader. Whenever it is clear a fragment isn't going to be visible (it is behind other objects) we can prematurely discard the fragment. A restriction on the fragment shader for early depth testing is that you shouldn't write to the fragment's depth value. If a fragment shader would write to its depth value, early depth testing is impossible; OpenGL won't be able to figure out the depth value beforehand.

glEnable(GL_DEPTH_TEST); The depth buffer is automatically created by the windowing system and stores its depth values as 16, 24 or 32 bit floats. In most systems you'll see a depth buffer with a precision of 24 bits. Once enabled, OpenGL automatically stores fragments their z-values in the depth buffer if they passed the depth test and discards fragments if they failed the depth test accordingly. If you have depth testing enabled you should also clear the depth buffer before each frame using GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT; otherwise you're stuck with the depth values from last frame: glClear(GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT | GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT); There are certain scenarios imaginable where you want to perform the depth test on all fragments and discard them accordingly, but not update the depth buffer. Basically, you're (temporarily) using a read-only depth buffer. OpenGL allows us to disable writing to the depth buffer by setting its depth mask to GL_FALSE: glDepthMask(GL_FALSE);
documents.s: When you don't need the depth texture

documents.t: To use the depth buffer later—typically for post-processing effects like Depth of Field, screen-space shadows, or fog—you cannot easily read from the default screen framebuffer. Instead, you need to render your scene into a custom Framebuffer Object (FBO) and attach a texture specifically to hold the depth data. Once rendered, that depth texture can be passed into a subsequent shader just like any normal color texture. Generate the depth texture: unsigned int depthTexture; glGenTextures(1, &depthTexture); glBindTexture(GL_TEXTURE_2D, depthTexture); // you must create a texture with a depth-specific format, such as GL_DEPTH_COMPONENT24 or GL_DEPTH_COMPONENT32F glTexImage2D(GL_TEXTURE_2D, 0, GL_DEPTH_COMPONENT24, 800, 600, 0, GL_DEPTH_COMPONENT, GL_FLOAT, NULL); glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_MIN_FILTER, GL_NEAREST); glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_MAG_FILTER, GL_NEAREST); glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_WRAP_S, GL_CLAMP_TO_EDGE); glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_WRAP_T, GL_CLAMP_TO_EDGE); Attach the Texture to a Custom Framebuffer: unsigned int fbo; glGenFramebuffers(1, &fbo); glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, fbo); // Attach the depth texture to the FBO glFramebufferTexture2D(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, GL_DEPTH_ATTACHMENT, GL_TEXTURE_2D, depthTexture, 0); // If you ONLY want depth and no color data in this FBO: // glDrawBuffer(GL_NONE); // glReadBuffer(GL_NONE); if (glCheckFramebufferStatus(GL_FRAMEBUFFER) != GL_FRAMEBUFFER_COMPLETE) std::cout << "ERROR::FRAMEBUFFER:: Framebuffer is not complete!" << std::endl; glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, 0); Render the Scene to the FBO: glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, fbo); glEnable(GL_DEPTH_TEST); glClear(GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT | GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT); RenderScene(); glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, 0); note Notice that we still had to use glEnable(GL_DEPTH_TEST), GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT. The only difference is we bound a custom fbo where we specified our custom depth attachment. Once you have written to the framebuffer, you're depth texture is ready to be bound to your shader program and read.
documents.s: When you need the depth texture

documents.t: OpenGL allows us to modify the comparison operators it uses for the depth test. glEnable(GL_DEPTH_TEST); // enable first glDepthFunc(GL_LESS); Function Description GL_ALWAYS The depth test always passes. GL_NEVER The depth test never passes. GL_LESS Passes if the fragment's depth value is less than the stored depth value. GL_EQUAL Passes if the fragment's depth value is equal to the stored depth value. GL_LEQUAL Passes if the fragment's depth value is less than or equal to the stored depth value. GL_GREATER Passes if the fragment's depth value is greater than the stored depth value. GL_NOTEQUAL Passes if the fragment's depth value is not equal to the stored depth value. GL_GEQUAL Passes if the fragment's depth value is greater than or equal to the stored depth value.

The depth value precision is non-linear, depth precision is highly dense near the near clipping plane and falls off exponentially as you approach the far clipping plane. To view the depth buffer, try rendering this to see that depth is not linear. #version 330 core out vec4 FragColor; float near = 0.1; float far = 100.0; float LinearizeDepth(float depth) { float z = depth * 2.0 - 1.0; // back to NDC return (2.0 * near * far) / (far + near - z * (far - near)); } void main() { float depth = LinearizeDepth(gl_FragCoord.z) / far; // divide by far for demonstration FragColor = vec4(vec3(depth), 1.0); // FragColor = vec4(vec3(gl_FragCoord.z), 1.0); }

A common visual artifact may occur when two planes or triangles are so closely aligned to each other that the depth buffer does not have enough precision to figure out which one of the two shapes is in front of the other. How to Fix Z-Fighting: Add a tiny offset: Manually nudge one of the geometry pieces slightly forward or backward in 3D space. Bring the near plane further away: Setting your projection matrix near plane to 0.1 instead of 0.001 dramatically saves depth precision. Use a higher bit depth buffer: Upgrade from a 16-bit to a 24-bit or 32-bit depth buffer.

The raw data stream fed into the pipeline from the CPU. This includes vertex positions, normals, texture coordinates (UVs), and colors stored in Vertex Buffer Objects (VBOs). std::vector<GLfloat> vertices = {0.5f, 0.5f, 0.0f, 0.5f, -0.5f, 0.0f, -0.5f, -0.5f, 0.0f, -0.5f, 0.5f, 0.0f}; std::vector<GLuint> indices = { 0, 1, 3, 1, 2, 3}; GLuint VAO, VBO, EBO; glGenVertexArrays(1, &VAO); glGenBuffers(1, &VBO); glGenBuffers(1, &EBO); // Start recording the VBO layout and EBO binding glBindVertexArray(VAO); // Vertex Buffer glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, VBO); glBufferData(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, vertices.size() * sizeof(GLfloat), vertices.data(), GL_STATIC_DRAW); // Element Buffer glBindBuffer(GL_ELEMENT_ARRAY_BUFFER, EBO); glBufferData(GL_ELEMENT_ARRAY_BUFFER, indices.size() * sizeof(GLuint), indices.data(), GL_STATIC_DRAW); // OpenGL should read the vertex array attributes // index -> location = 0 // size -> 3 components // type-> float component // normalized -> nope // stride -> Byte offset between consecutive generic vertex attributes. In this case: x,y,z * sizeof(float) // pointer-> Offset of the first component of the first generic vertex attribute in the array, in this case offset 0. // https://registry.khronos.org/OpenGL-Refpages/gl4/html/glVertexAttribPointer.xhtml // void glVertexAttribPointer(GLuint index, GLint size, GLenum type, GLboolean normalized, GLsizei stride, const void * pointer); glVertexAttribPointer(0, 3, GL_FLOAT, GL_FALSE, 3 * sizeof(float), (void*)0); // To use non-array data, call glDisableVertexAttribArray. // To use array data, call glEnableVertexAttribArray. In this case, it's array data. glEnableVertexAttribArray(0); // Stop recording glBindVertexArray(0); glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, 0); int render() { // ... glBindVertexArray(VAO); glDrawElements(GL_TRIANGLES, 6, GL_UNSIGNED_INT, 0); glBindVertexArray(0); // ... }

Executes once for each vertex. Its primary job is to transform vertices from local object space into Clip Space using your Matrix transformations (MVP). It can also pass data (like texture coordinates) down the line. #version 450 core layout (location = 0) in vec3 aPos; // Input from VTX uniform mat4 u_MVP; void main() { gl_Position = u_MVP * vec4(aPos, 1.0); }

The first part of the tessellation process. It determines how much to subdivide a patch of geometry by setting tessellation levels (gl_TessLevelOuter and gl_TessLevelInner). #version 450 core layout (vertices = 3) out; // Output 3 vertices per patch void main() { // Pass coordinate data through gl_out[gl_InvocationID].gl_Position = gl_in[gl_InvocationID].gl_Position; // Set tessellation levels (how fine the split is) if (gl_InvocationID == 0) { gl_TessLevelInner[0] = 4.0; // Subdivisions inside gl_TessLevelOuter[0] = 4.0; // Subdivisions on edges gl_TessLevelOuter[1] = 4.0; gl_TessLevelOuter[2] = 4.0; } }

Calculates the actual positions of the new vertices generated by the tessellation engine using barycentric coordinates (gl_TessCoord). #version 450 core layout (triangles, equal_spacing, ccw) in; void main() { // Interpolate the position of the new vertex across the triangle patch // glPatchParameteri(GL_PATCH_VERTICES, 3); // therefore, gl_in has 3 vertices vec3 p0 = gl_in[0].gl_Position.xyz; vec3 p1 = gl_in[1].gl_Position.xyz; vec3 p2 = gl_in[2].gl_Position.xyz; // gl_TessCoord holds barycentric coordinates (u, v, w) vec3 finalPos = gl_TessCoord.x * p0 + gl_TessCoord.y * p1 + gl_TessCoord.z * p2; gl_Position = vec4(finalPos, 1.0); } To render patches via C++, change your draw call from GL_TRIANGLES to GL_PATCHES: void render() { // ... glPatchParameteri(GL_PATCH_VERTICES, 3); glDrawElements(GL_PATCHES, 6, GL_UNSIGNED_INT, 0); // ... } note glPatchParameteri(GL_PATCH_VERTICES, 3); GL_PATCH_VERTICES is set to 3, so in the TES, gl_in will have 3 vertices/elements info Barycentric coordinates are a system that specifies the location of a point inside or around a geometric shape (a simplex, such as a triangle or tetrahedron) using relative weights or masses assigned to the shape's vertices

Takes full primitives and can generate new vertices or primitives using EmitVertex() and EndPrimitive(). #version 450 core layout (triangles) in; layout (triangle_strip, max_vertices = 3) out; void main() { // Example: Simply pass the original triangle straight through for(int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { gl_Position = gl_in[i].gl_Position; EmitVertex(); // Construct a vertex } EndPrimitive(); }

glViewport(0, 0, 800, 600); // mapping area to screen space glCullFace(GL_BACK); // Cull back-facing triangles glEnable(GL_CULL_FACE); // Enable face culling glPolygonMode(GL_FRONT_AND_BACK, GL_FILL); // GL_FILL / GL_LINE / GL_POINT
Determines color of the pixel Runs once per pixel. #version 450 core out vec4 FragColor; void main() { FragColor = vec4(1.0f, 0.0f, 0.0f, 1.0f); // A nice bright red screen :P }

Like the Rasterizer, this is a fixed-function testing and blending ground handled via API commands. Here is the chronological order of the standard tests: Scissor Test: Checks if the fragment lies within a specific rectangular window defined by glScissor. Stencil Test: Compares the fragment's stencil value against the stencil buffer. (First) Depth Test: Compares the fragment’s zzz value against the depth buffer. (Second) Blending: Combines the fragment's color with the color already in the framebuffer (used for transparency). Dithering: A historical technique to arrange pixels to simulate more colors on low-color displays (rarely noticeable on modern hardware). Logical Operations: Applies bitwise operations between the fragment color and framebuffer color (rarely used today). note While the above is the logical order of the pipeline, modern GPUs have an optimization trick called Early-Z (or Early Fragment Testing). If your fragment shader isn't doing anything crazy, the GPU will actually run the Stencil and Depth tests before the Fragment Shader even runs. If you use the discard keyword in your fragment shader, or if you manually modify the depth value inside the shader (gl_FragDepth), the GPU is forced to disable Early-Z and revert to the standard order, because it can't know the true depth of the pixel until the shader finishes executing. glEnable(GL_DEPTH_TEST); // Discard fragments hidden behind closer objects glDepthFunc(GL_LESS); glEnable(GL_BLEND); // Alpha maps for transparency glBlendFunc(GL_SRC_ALPHA, GL_ONE_MINUS_SRC_ALPHA); // Clear buffers at start of loop glClear(GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT | GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT | GL_STENCIL_BUFFER_BIT);

Bind textures, write to them, paint a picture, do math, etc. You can use shaders to do any computation. The Compute Shader sits completely outside the traditional graphics rendering pipeline #version 450 core layout (local_size_x = 16, local_size_y = 16, local_size_z = 1) in; layout(rgba32f, binding = 0) writeonly uniform image2D imgOutput; void main() { ivec2 pixelCoords = ivec2(gl_GlobalInvocationID.xy); vec4 pixelColor = vec4(1.0, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0); imageStore(imgOutput, pixelCoords, pixelColor); }

Stencil testing is used to discard fragments based on a configurable stencil mask. It allows you to mask out specific areas of the screen to achieve advanced visual effects like outlines, portals, mirrors, or shadows.

The stencil buffer is a non-visible 2D buffer that matches the width and height of your screen window, exactly like the depth buffer. Instead of storing depth values, each pixel in the stencil buffer stores an integer value (usually an 8-bit unsigned integer, providing values between 0 and 255). Stencil testing is done in screen space after the fragment shader has run, but right before the depth test. During a stencil test, a fragment's configured stencil reference value is compared against the stencil value currently stored in the buffer. If the test passes, the fragment is kept for further processing (like the depth test). If it fails, the fragment is immediately discarded. note Because the stencil test happens before the depth test, you can discard fragments early based on stencil masks, saving performance on subsequent depth calculations. note Unlike the depth buffer, which stores continuous floating-point approximations, the stencil buffer stores exact integer values. This makes it perfect for precise binary masking or integer-based counting operations per pixel.

glEnable(GL_STENCIL_TEST); The stencil buffer is automatically managed by the windowing system alongside the depth buffer (often combined into a single GL_DEPTH24_STENCIL8 internal format). Once enabled, OpenGL compares fragments against the stencil buffer. If you have stencil testing enabled, you should clear the stencil buffer before each frame using GL_STENCIL_BUFFER_BIT to reset your masks; otherwise, you'll carry over masking states from the previous frame: glClear(GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT | GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT | GL_STENCIL_BUFFER_BIT); Just like the depth mask, OpenGL allows us to enable or disable writing to the stencil buffer. You can pass a bitmask to glStencilMask to control which bits are allowed to be written to the buffer: // Everything written to the stencil buffer is disabled (Read-only) glStencilMask(0x00); // Each bit is written to the stencil buffer as-is (Write enabled) glStencilMask(0xFF);
documents.s: When you don't need the stencil texture

documents.t: To use the stencil buffer later—typically for advanced post-processing effects like screen-space masks, local rendering layers, or custom UI masking—you need to render your scene into a custom Framebuffer Object (FBO) and attach a texture capable of holding stencil data. Because depth and stencil data are usually bound together on hardware, you will typically create a single depth-stencil texture.
